mainText:
**The Great American Wanderer**
When Louis L’Amour’s high school class was graduating, he was curiously absent—he was in Singapore, just one of many stops in his roving life. In today’s American Artifacts, Dispatch reporter Grayson Logue reflects on L’Amour’s memoir, which recalls the author’s travels and names his greatest companions: books.

Elsewhere in this newsletter, you’ll find an essay suggestion from yours truly, recommendations from Dispatch senior editor John McCormack, a book review by Celine Lombardi, and a Work of the Week by Dispatch member Kevin Hodge.

On the site today, contributing writer Leah Libresco Sargeant discusses how coding with the help of AI might help improve the internet. She expresses excitement about the future of vibe coding—code written in plain English through AI prompting—because it could give ordinary people more control over their digital lives.

We also have Nadya Williams writing on a new book titled *Fairies: A History*, contemplating the apparent reenchantment of our society. She notes that while the early aughts marked the heyday of New Atheism, the 2020s have emerged as a time of reenchantment and new works of apologetics for religious belief.

Finally, this weekend’s “Where I’m From” installment comes from Hans Zeiger, a native of Puyallup, Washington. He shares, “You’re likely to pass through Puyallup if you’re heading to the glacier-bound volcano, and of all the many grand views of the peak the indigenous people called Tahoma, my favorites come from old photos showing it as the monumental backdrop for the acres and acres of daffodil fields that once spread across parts of the valley floor.”

**Louis L’Amour’s Memoir**
“It was May 14. In a few days my class back in Jamestown, North Dakota would be graduating from high school, and I was in Singapore.” That’s how Louis L’Amour opens his memoir, *Education of a Wandering Man*, completed shortly before his death in 1988 and published the following year.

The 20th-century sage of the American West quit school at the age of 15 to seek work, stating that “more important was that school was interfering with my education.” He recounts his journey from adolescence through early adulthood in the 1920s and ’30s, describing his “knock-about” years where he hopped railway lines across the country, took on odd jobs, worked as a caretaker of a remote mine, a cattle skinner, a circus hand, and a prizefighter. He became a merchant marine, crossing oceans between ports as distant as Singapore, England, China, and Egypt.

The first encounter I had with L’Amour’s tales wasn’t through his books, which have sold hundreds of millions of copies worldwide, but through John Wayne movies. *Hondo*, based on a L’Amour short story, was a favorite, and I watched it repeatedly with my grandfather growing up. I only read a few of L’Amour’s novels until I discovered *Education* a few years ago, captivated not only by his roving early life but also by his profound journey of reading.

Indeed, *Education* isn’t a traditional memoir, nor is it solely about the travels that would inform much of L’Amour’s writing. It’s a book about the reading life, “a story of an adventure in education, pursued not under the best of conditions.” Books were a constant presence, fueling his relentless yearning to learn: “I have read my books by many lights, hoarding their beauty, their wit or wisdom against the dark days when I would have no book, nor a place to read.”

Included in the memoir are photos of L’Amour’s handwritten lists of books and plays read each year from 1930 to 1935, as well as 1937, showcasing he completed over 100 works annually. “A spectacularly serendipitous reader, he enlists us in the joys of random reading,” notes the late Daniel Boorstin, in the introduction to the 1989 edition of *Educated*. He found joy in unprogrammed juxtapositions of topics and figures, quoting Robert W. Service and William Butler Yeats in the same breath.

Initially, L’Amour faced challenges finding books on the road, but by the end of his life, access to reading material improved significantly. “Education is available to anyone within reach of a library, a post office, or even a newsstand,” he writes. He dismisses excuses regarding lack of time to read, sharing that during one year, he read twenty-five books while waiting in various places.

Today’s challenge isn’t obtainin
